elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose CNMBert which stands for zh-CN Pinyin Multi-mask Bert Model as a solution to this issue.

elements.sent-2411-014: We treat the this as a Fill-Mask task.
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically, we extend the number of mask tokens in the simple BERT model\citep{devlin2019bertpretrainingdeepbidirectional to match the number of letters in the Hanyu Pinyin's alphabet.}
elements.sent-2411-016: And, each letter is mapped to a distinct mask token (e.g., The letter 'a' will be mapped to mask token [LETTER\_A] ), then the model predicts the corresponding mask tokens, thereby generating the translation of the pinyin abbreviation.
elements.sent-2411-017: Since BERT is pre-trained on the Fill-Mask task, this ensures consistency between pre-training and downstream tasks.

elements.sent-2411-019: The results indicate that CNMBert better understands and utilizes the information conveyed by Hanyu Pinyin Abbreviations.

elements.sent-2411-021: In this section, we will discuss related work associated with our model, as well as work on Hanyu Pinyin translation.
elements.sent-2411-022: The original BERT model used WordPiece tokenizer\citep{wu2016googlesneuralmachinetranslation to segment the input into WordPiece tokens, in which case a single word could be split into several smaller pieces and when training on the Fill-Mask task, those words may only have part of their tokens masked.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For Chinese, the original BERT model segments input by characters, with each character mapping to an individual token.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, this approach can also result in only part of a multisyllabic word being masked.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address the issue of only parts of words being masked, the Google team proposed Whole Word Masking (WWM).
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach allows the model to mask entire words' tokens, making it easier for the model to predict the masked content\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1810-04805 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: For the Chinese BERT model, Cui Yiming et al. using LTP, a Chinese text segmentation tool\citep{che-etal-2021-n , to segment the input text into several words.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Based on this, they developed the Chinese-WWM-BERT model for Chinese text\citep{cui-etal-2020-revisiting .}
elements.sent-2411-029: And experimental results have shown that the BERT model using WWM outperforms the original BERT model, achieving state-of-the-art performance on most tasks.. \begin{CJK* {UTF8 {gbsn.}}}

elements.sent-2411-035: In this section, we will provide a detailed description of the architecture of our CNMBert and explain how we attempt to enable the BERT model to understand and leverage the information contained in Hanyu Pinyin Abbreviations.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the MLM task, 15\% of the characters in the input sentence are replaced with the [MASK] token, and the model is required to predict the masked words based on the surrounding context.
elements.sent-2411-037: Building upon the Whole Word Mask (WWM) approach, we modified the masking logic.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, we expanded the number of mask tokens so that each character is mapped to a distinct mask token.
elements.sent-2411-039: During training, our model first selects the words to be masked.
elements.sent-2411-040: Then, using the pypinyin package\footnote{\url{https://github.com/mozillazg/python-pinyin , retrieves the first letter of the Hanyu Pinyin for each character.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This letter is mapped to the corresponding mask token, which serves as the masked token for that character, as illustrated in Table 2.
elements.sent-2411-042: Since CNMBert pre-training task aligns with the downstream tasks, we have opted to discard this adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-043: In our model's pre-training phase, only 10\% of the selected words are replaced with random characters, while the remaining 90\% selected words follow the multi-mask strategy.
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, we increased the probability of masking multisyllabic words rather than individual characters.
elements.sent-2411-045: This strategy encourages the model to focus more on training with multisyllabic words, thereby enhancing its prediction capability for such words.. \begin{CJK* {UTF8 {gbsn.}}}

elements.sent-2411-094: In this paper, to address the problem of Hanyu Pinyin abbreviation to character conversion task, we improved the traditional BERT model architecture and masking logic, proposing CNMBert.
elements.sent-2411-095: Using our constructed test set, we validated that this model outperforms other GPT models in terms of accuracy for Hanyu Pinyin abbreviation to character conversion task.
elements.sent-2411-096: CNMBert introduces a novel multi-mask and MoE-based approach, which can be adapted for solving the pinyin abbreviation problem and can help people better understand the meaning of Hanyu Pinyin abbreviations, offering potential improved solutions for tasks such as text sentiment analysis and Chinese spelling correction(CSC).. \bibliographystyle{acl_natbib .}

elements.sent-2411-097: The task of converting hanyu pinyin abbreviations to Chinese characters is a significant branch within the domain of Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC) behind many downstream applications.
elements.sent-2411-098: This task is typically one of text-length alignment and seems easy to solve; however, due to the limited informational content in pinyin abbreviations, achieving accurate conversion is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this paper, we treat this as a Fill-Mask task then propose CNMBert, which stands for zh-CN Pinyin Multi-mask Bert Model, as a solution to this issue.
elements.sent-2411-100: CNMBert surpasses fine-tuning GPT models, achieving a 60.56 MRR score and 51.09 accuracy on a 10,229-sample pinyin abbreviation test dataset, providing a viable solution to this task.

elements.sent-2411-189: In this paper, to address the problem of pinyin abbreviation to character conversion task, we improved the traditional BERT model architecture and masking logic, proposing CNMBert.
elements.sent-2411-190: Using our constructed test dataset, we validated that this model outperforms other GPT models in terms of accuracy for pinyin abbreviation to character conversion task.
elements.sent-2411-191: CNMBert introduces a novel multi-mask and MoE-based approach, which can be adapted for solving the pinyin abbreviation problem and can help people better understand the meaning of pinyin abbreviations, offering potential improved solutions for tasks such as text sentiment analysis and Chinese spelling correction (CSC).. \bibliographystyle{acl_natbib .}
elements.sent-2411-192: The task of converting hanyu pinyin abbreviations to Chinese characters is a significant branch within the domain of Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC).
elements.sent-2411-193: It plays an important role in many downstream applications like named entity recognition and sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-194: This task is typically one of text-length alignment and seems easy to solve; however, due to the limited information content in pinyin abbreviations, achieving accurate conversion is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-195: By introducing a multi-mask strategy and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers, CNMBert outperforms fine-tuned GPT models and ChatGPT-4o with a 61.53 MRR score and 51.86 accuracy on a 10,373-sample test dataset.

elements.sent-2411-198: One of the most notable manifestations of this phenomenon is the “B-culture” on Douyin \footnote{\url{https://www.zhihu.com/question/269016377/answer/2654824753 .}}
elements.sent-2411-199: In an effort to maintain a positive and healthy atmosphere on the platform, Douyin is hypercorrecting by implementing numerous ridiculous prohibited words.

elements.sent-2411-349: In Table V, we present the overall MRR scores of different models on the test dataset, along with the MRR scores for predicting monosyllabic words and polysyllabic words separately.
elements.sent-2411-350: We found that the ability to predict pinyin abbreviations of GPT models without fine-tuning is quite limited.
elements.sent-2411-351: After fine-tuning, the performance of Qwen2.5-14b has significantly improved, but it still does not surpass CNMBERT.
elements.sent-2411-352: Additionally, GPT models do not utilize the MLM pretraining task, which further diminishes their performance.
elements.sent-2411-353: For CNMBERT with multi-mask strategy, the experimental results indicate that it can effectively infer the meaning of pinyin abbreviations, with its performance significantly outperforming the others.
elements.sent-2411-354: Additionally, the results of the ablation experiment show that the multi-mask strategy and the inclusion of MoE layers significantly enhance its performance.
elements.sent-2411-355: However, when there is too little contextual information or the pinyin abbreviation sequences are too long, CNMBERT's performance may experience a sharp decline.
elements.sent-2411-356: Moreover, polysyllabic words consist of multiple Chinese characters, and as the length of the pinyin abbreviation increases, the number of possible outcomes grows exponentially.
elements.sent-2411-357: This significantly increases the difficulty of making accurate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-358: While autoregressive models like Qwen-FT can generate text sequentially, autoencoder models like CNMBERT lack this capability, leading to weaker performance on longer pinyin abbreviation sequences.
elements.sent-2411-359: As shown in Fig. 3, CNMBERT's performance declines more sharply as the length of the target word increases than GPT models.

elements.sent-2411-362: In addition, different experts process mask tokens belonging to separate clusters, indicating that the experts are capable of capturing the feature variations among mask tokens and performing targeted processing accordingly.. \par.
elements.sent-2411-363: In this paper, to address the problem of converting pinyin abbreviations to Chinese characters, we improved the traditional BERT model architecture and masking logic, proposing CNMBERT.
elements.sent-2411-364: Using our constructed test dataset, we validated that this model outperforms other GPT models and the baseline model in terms of accuracy for the pinyin abbreviation to character conversion task.
elements.sent-2411-365: CNMBERT introduces a novel multi-mask strategy and MoE-based approach, which can be adapted for solving the pinyin abbreviation problem and can help people better understand the meaning of pinyin abbreviations, offering potentially improved solutions for tasks such as named entity recognition and sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-366: To improve the performance of this model, future work will be performed to explore enhanced strategies for resolving pinyin ambiguity through using the multi-mask strategy and expanding the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-367: The proposed CNMBERT architecture will be also applied to the task of translating abbreviations in other languages, such as translating English acronyms.
